policy_statement: Assess training needs for staff to ensure informed response to extreme weather events.
mechanism_description: Assessing staff training needs helps improve informed responses to extreme weather events, strengthening institutional adaptive capacity.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard → ↑ institutional preparedness/response capacity → ↓ exposure/sensitivity → ↓ climate impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: The institution evaluates staff competencies and training gaps and then targets training/skills development for extreme-weather response roles and protocols.  
(2) Process: Increased knowledge, situational awareness, decision quality, and procedural compliance improves emergency management performance (planning, coordination, communication, and rapid response).  
(3) Outcome: Reduced disruption, damages, and safety risks during extreme weather events (i.e., enhanced adaptive capacity and resilience of services and operations).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved capacity to anticipate and respond to extreme weather, reducing harm and service disruption during climate hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazards (extreme weather) are better managed through improved staff readiness, which lowers impacts—an adaptation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while better-trained staff could incidentally support mitigation programs, the stated and typical dominant effect of this mechanism is preparedness/response to hazards, not emissions reduction or resource efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: if training were explicitly about energy management or emissions accounting, mitigation/resource-efficiency could dominate; here the mechanism explicitly targets extreme weather response, so adaptation dominates.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy fitting “staff training needs assessment → institutional adaptive capacity” centers on building organizational capacity to cope with hazards, consistently aligning with adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: specific adaptation subtypes (e.g., emergency management, continuity of operations, public health preparedness) depend on policy-level details and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the assessed training needs are primarily for mitigation implementation (e.g., fleet electrification maintenance, building retro-commissioning) rather than hazard response, the dominant pathway could shift away from Adaptation; in the provided mechanism description it is clearly extreme-weather response.
co_benefits: Improved general emergency preparedness and occupational safety for non-climate incidents; enhanced staff competency and service continuity overall.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be coded as an incentive-program or regulatory-mandate if paired with funded training or mandatory certification requirements, but the text only specifies assessment of needs (planning/capacity assessment).
